raw_response:
```json
{
  "communicative_function": "The statement is reporting a personal observation or testimonial, describing a perceived cause-and-effect relationship between an unspecified action ('when he does so') and a physical outcome (improved respiration and walking). It functions as an anecdotal claim of efficacy.",
  "implicit_claims": [
    "There is a 'he' whose identity and action are presumed to be known from prior context.",
    "The speaker has the ability to reliably observe and assess changes in respiration and walking.",
    "The improvement is causally linked to 'he' performing some action.",
    "The improvement is genuine and not a placebo, coincidence, or misperception.",
    "The word 'remarkable' implies the improvement exceeded normal expectations.",
    "The speaker's astonishment implies the outcome was unexpected, possibly suggesting prior skepticism."
  ],
  "coherence_assessment": "The statement is internally coherent as a sentence but is deeply context-dependent. Without knowing who 'he' is, what action was performed, or the baseline condition of the subject, the statement is logically incomplete. As a standalone principle for AI ethics guidance, it is entirely incoherent — it appears to be a fragment of a medical or observational narrative misapplied to an AI-ethics framing.",
  "conditions_for_usefulness": [
    "The referent ('he') and the action ('does so') are clearly defined by surrounding context.",
    "The observation is made by a qualified observer using reliable measurement methods.",
    "The claim is part of a structured case study or documented medical/scientific account.",
    "It is used to illustrate the importance of human observation or caregiver attention as a value AI should support."
  ],
  "conditions_for_misleading": [
    "The statement is used out of context to imply scientific or medical validation without evidence.",
    "The 'remarkable improvement' is subjective and not corroborated by objective measurement.",
    "The action ('does so') is left undefined, allowing the statement to be retrofitted to justify any intervention.",
    "It is presented as an AI-ethics principle, which it clearly is not, thereby lending false authority to an anecdotal observation.",
    "The statement is used to promote a specific product, treatment, or behavior without disclosing the full context."
  ]
}
```

parsed_json.communicative_function: The statement is reporting a personal observation or testimonial, describing a perceived cause-and-effect relationship between an unspecified action ('when he does so') and a physical outcome (improved respiration and walking). It functions as an anecdotal claim of efficacy.
implicit_claims: There is a 'he' whose identity and action are presumed to be known from prior context.
The speaker has the ability to reliably observe and assess changes in respiration and walking.
The improvement is causally linked to 'he' performing some action.
The improvement is genuine and not a placebo, coincidence, or misperception.
The word 'remarkable' implies the improvement exceeded normal expectations.
The speaker's astonishment implies the outcome was unexpected, possibly suggesting prior skepticism.
parsed_json.coherence_assessment: The statement is internally coherent as a sentence but is deeply context-dependent. Without knowing who 'he' is, what action was performed, or the baseline condition of the subject, the statement is logically incomplete. As a standalone principle for AI ethics guidance, it is entirely incoherent — it appears to be a fragment of a medical or observational narrative misapplied to an AI-ethics framing.
conditions_for_usefulness: The referent ('he') and the action ('does so') are clearly defined by surrounding context.
The observation is made by a qualified observer using reliable measurement methods.
The claim is part of a structured case study or documented medical/scientific account.
It is used to illustrate the importance of human observation or caregiver attention as a value AI should support.
conditions_for_misleading: The statement is used out of context to imply scientific or medical validation without evidence.
The 'remarkable improvement' is subjective and not corroborated by objective measurement.
The action ('does so') is left undefined, allowing the statement to be retrofitted to justify any intervention.
It is presented as an AI-ethics principle, which it clearly is not, thereby lending false authority to an anecdotal observation.
The statement is used to promote a specific product, treatment, or behavior without disclosing the full context.
